title: Learn About Lottery Mathematics
subtitle: Understanding the true odds behind lottery games

probability.title: What is Probability?
probability.text: Probability is the mathematical likelihood that an event will occur. It's expressed as a number between 0 and 1, where 0 means impossible and 1 means certain. For example, the probability of flipping heads on a fair coin is 0.5 (or 50%), since there are two equally likely outcomes.
probability.exampleText: When you roll a fair six-sided die:
probability.exampleItem2: The probability of rolling a 3 is 1/6 ≈ 16.67%
probability.exampleItem3: The probability of rolling any number is equally likely

combinations.title: Combinations and Permutations
combinations.text: When calculating lottery odds, we need to understand how many different ways items can be selected and arranged. In most lotteries, the order of numbers doesn't matter, so we use combinations.
combinations.exampleText: In a typical lottery where you pick 6 numbers from 49:
combinations.exampleItem1: The number of possible combinations is approximately 13.98 million
combinations.exampleItem2: Your chance of winning with one ticket is 1 in 13.98 million

expectedValue.title: Expected Value
expectedValue.text: Expected value is the average amount you expect to win or lose per bet in the long run. For lotteries, the expected value is always negative, meaning you lose money on average.
expectedValue.exampleItem5: Over time, you'd expect to break even, but real lotteries are worse!

houseFavor.title: Why Lotteries Always Favor the House
houseFavor.text: Real-world lotteries keep a percentage of ticket sales (typically 30-50%) before paying out prizes. This is called the "house advantage." Here's what this means:
houseFavor.item1: $40-50 goes to the lottery organizer (state, company, etc.)
houseFavor.item3: The remaining funds go to causes (education, infrastructure, etc.)
houseFavor.item4: Result: Players get back only 50-60 cents for every dollar spent

comparison.title: Lottery vs Investing: A 30-Year Comparison
comparison.text: What if you invested the money you'd spend on lottery tickets instead? Let's compare spending $20 per week on lottery tickets versus investing that same amount.
comparison.investNote: Based on ~7% average annual return (S&P 500 historical)
comparison.takeaway: The difference: Over 30 years, the lottery player loses about $15,600 while the investor gains about $70,800. That's an $86,400 gap from the same $20 per week.

gamblersFallacy.title: The "Gambler's Fallacy"
gamblersFallacy.text: Many people believe that past results affect future outcomes in independent events. This is false. Each lottery draw is independent, and previous outcomes don't influence future ones.
gamblersFallacy.misconception1: "7 was drawn last week, so it's less likely this week"
gamblersFallacy.reality1: Each number has exactly the same chance every draw
gamblersFallacy.misconception2: "I'm due for a win after losing many times"
gamblersFallacy.reality2: Past losses don't increase your chance of winning
gamblersFallacy.misconception3: "These numbers are 'hot' so they're more likely"
gamblersFallacy.reality3: Frequently drawn numbers are just as likely as rare ones

largeNumbers.title: The Law of Large Numbers
largeNumbers.text: As you play more games, your actual results converge toward the theoretical probability. However, this doesn't mean you'll "eventually win" - it means you'll eventually lose even more money!
largeNumbers.item1: Play 1 lottery ticket: You might win once in a lifetime
largeNumbers.item2: Play 100 tickets: Your odds improve, but expected loss is still your full investment
largeNumbers.item3: Play 1,000 tickets: You're closer to the statistical average (losing ~50-60% of money spent)
largeNumbers.item4: The more you play, the closer your losses approach the mathematical expectation

lessons.title: Smart Financial Lessons
lessons.text: Understanding lottery mathematics teaches us valuable lessons about probability and economic decisions:
lessons.riskRewardText: Always evaluate the odds versus the potential reward. In lotteries, the risk-to-reward ratio is extremely unfavorable compared to other investments.
lessons.statThinkingText: Make decisions based on data and probability, not hope or superstition. This applies to investing, insurance, and major life decisions.
lessons.longTermPlanningText: Lottery winnings are unreliable. Building wealth through regular saving and investing (even small amounts) is much more reliable than hoping for a jackpot.
lessons.psychologyLossText: Understand how our brains trick us into poor decisions. We overestimate small probabilities and underestimate our losses when they're spread over time.

cta.title: See It for Yourself
cta.text: The best way to understand lottery odds is to experience them firsthand. Try our interactive lottery simulator and watch how many draws it takes to match your numbers.

responsible.title: Responsible Gaming
responsible.text: If you ever feel that lottery play is becoming a problem, or if it's affecting your financial health, please reach out for help:
responsible.remember: Lottery tickets should only be used as entertainment with money you can afford to lose. Never rely on them for financial planning.
